text.fullText:
I write to strongly oppose the U.S. Environmental Protection Agency’s (EPA) proposed rule to again
improperly weaken regulations related to Section 401 of the Clean Water Act (CWA or the Act). Section
401 represents an important tool used by states and Tribes to protect state and Tribal water resources
from the impacts of federally permitted development. Section 401 is a linchpin of the cooperative
federalism structure envisioned by Congress when it passed the Act in 1972 and is specifically designed
to empower states and Tribes. The proposed rule would dramatically undercut the role provided to
states and Tribes by the Act in protecting their waters.
 
The proposed rule largely recycles a rule issued in 2020 that upended almost fifty years of strong state
and Tribal authority to ensure that projects such as dams, pipelines, highways, datacenters, and other
projects are protective of state and Tribal waters and the important services they provide people and
communities. The harm done by the 2020 rule was substantially corrected in 2023 by a revised rule that
restored the proper role for states and Tribes envisioned by the Act and the long-standing regulatory
structure that stood in place since 1971. In doing so, it also updated the rule to clarify that state and
Tribal reviews were appropriately limited to water related requirements and that there was a clear and
timely process for applicants to follow.
 
The proposed rule is an unnecessary retread of the rejected 2020 rule. Of foremost
concern, it would violate the Act by rejecting its clear meaning and overturning Supreme Court
precedent confirming that meaning by limiting the scope of certification from the activity as a whole to
the “discharge from a point source” of a project. This would dramatically limit the ability of states and
Tribes to look at the totality of potential impacts and harms to waters from a project. For instance, it
could eliminate or undermine state and Tribes from being able to put in place protective measures so
that rivers and streams have adequate flow, habitat is protected, and designated uses like fishing and
recreation enjoyed by the public are not disturbed or destroyed.
It is hard to see this proposal as anything thing other than an unwarranted assault on clean water and
state and Tribal rights. Any concerns, which we feel were misplaced at the time, about uncertainty
around timing or process were addressed with the changes in the 2023 rule. The 2023 rule also makes it
clear that certification, conditions, and denial must regard water quality requirements. The
combined result of these two proposed rules will be far fewer opportunities for state and Tribes to
protect their waters under Section 401 and far weaker options for them to do so in the limited number
of times it is triggered. This will lead to more water pollution, more destruction of critical aquatic
resources, more risk of flooding and other disasters, more threats to drinking water and public health,
the loss of habitat, deprivation of recreational and other opportunities, and other negative
consequences for our waters and the people – which is all of us – that rely on them.
We strongly urge you not to move forward with this ill-conceived and unnecessary rule and keep the
existing 2023 rule in place.
Sincerely,
Marie Noel
